title: Sun-dried tomato scones
subtitle: Savoury scones with intense tomato and a hint of basil
excerpt: Oil-packed sun-dried tomatoes folded into a plain scone dough give a concentrated, slightly sweet tomato flavour in every slice. A little of the tomato oil goes into the dough in place of some butter, adding richness. These work well as a side to soup or alongside cheese. They bake in 12 minutes and the whole process takes under 30 minutes.
sourceNotes: Savoury scones appear throughout British and Irish baking from the nineteenth century, with cheese scones the most documented form. Sun-dried tomato scones became common in British artisan bakeries in the early 2000s as Mediterranean ingredients became more widely available. Rachel Allen and James Martin both include savoury scone variants in their television baking series of that era.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked scones for up to 1 month. Reheat from frozen at 180 degrees C fan for 8 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best eaten on the day. The cut rounds can be chilled overnight and baked the following morning.

glossaryTerms.definition: Savoury scones follow the same rubbing-in method as sweet scones, but the cold butter is critical: it creates flaky pockets of steam during baking that give the scone its lift and layered texture. Work quickly with cold hands, stop as soon as the dough comes together, and handle it as little as possible. Overworking savoury scone dough after the liquid is added develops the gluten and produces a dense, bread-like texture rather than a tender, crumbly one.

content.text: is fast and light. Once the liquid is in, stop as soon as the dough comes together; five more seconds of stirring is too many. The mixture should look rough and just-held-together when you tip it out.

Use oil-packed sun-dried tomatoes rather than dry-packed ones: they are softer, easier to chop and add flavour to the reserved oil. Drain them well and press in kitchen paper before chopping; too much brine in the dough will make the scones wet and heavy.

items.prepNote: oil-packed, drained, patted dry and roughly chopped
from the tomato jar if possible

content.text: Heat the oven to 210 degrees C fan and place a baking tray inside to heat. Combine the 250 g flour, 1 tsp baking powder and 0.5 tsp salt. Rub in the 40 g cold butter until the mixture resembles breadcrumbs. Stir in the parmesan (if using) and 0.5 tsp dried basil. Beat together the egg, 90 ml cold milk and 1 tbsp oil. Pour into the flour mixture and add the chopped tomatoes. Mix with a fork until just combined.

Turn onto a lightly floured surface and pat out to 2.5 cm thick. Cut into rounds with a 6 cm cutter, pressing straight down. Place on the hot baking tray. Brush the tops with a little milk. Bake for 11 to 14 minutes until risen and deep golden brown on top, with a firm base that sounds hollow when tapped. Cool on a rack.

items.problem: Wet, heavy scones with a doughy centre
items.solution: The sun-dried tomatoes had too much oil or brine on them. Pat them thoroughly dry between sheets of kitchen paper before chopping, and drain well in a sieve. The dough should feel similar in consistency to a sweet scone dough.

The dough was too wet or the baking tray was not preheated. Use a hot tray; the bottom heat sets the base before the sides have time to spread. If the dough looks very loose, pat it out thicker and cut fewer scones.

items.problem: Tomato pieces fall out when the scone is broken
items.solution: The pieces were too large. Chop the sun-dried tomatoes finely (about 5 mm dice) so they are distributed through the dough rather than sitting in it as loose inclusions.
